text: The surgeon uses a Cartier grasping instrument in the left hand and a vessel sealer extend in the right hand. The third robotic arm is actively elevating the liver and hiatus. The dissection of the hernia sac begins at the twelve o'clock position on the hiatus, with the peritoneum incised and the proper plane identified. An assistant uses a paddle babcock through a non-robotic air seal trocar to aid in blunt dissection, freeing the hernia sac from the mediastinum.

The surgeon begins dissection anteriorly, dividing the gastrophrenic and esophageal phrenic ligaments, progressing towards the base of the right crus before shifting to the left crus. The assistant adjusts the position to maintain tension on the hernia sac. The third robotic arm actively aids in exposure, revealing the descending thoracic aorta centrally on the screen. The hernia sac is carefully divided along the inside of the pleura to minimize deep peritonealization.

The surgeon attempts to develop a retroesophageal window by connecting the base of the left cruz to the right, but switches to dividing the short gastric vessels due to the size of the parasophageal hernia. The assistant uses a Babcock to grasp the anterior wall of the stomach, while the surgeon retracts the gastro-splenic omentum with the left hand. The vessel sealer, a bipolar device with a cutting blade, is used to limit collateral thermal spread and cut tissue. A trainee at the dual console is learning to position for taking down the short gastric vessels.

The surgeon uses a robotic console to guide the trainee in taking down the short gastric vessels, maintaining a distance of approximately one centimeter from the greater curvature of the stomach to prevent thermal injury. The assistant actively re-grasps the stomach to aid in the division of the short gastrics, facilitating tension-free mobilization of the fundus for a Nissen fundoplication.

The surgeon observes the stretched short gastric vessels in a patient with a large parasophageal hernia, noting the increased space between the fundus and spleen. The camera port is positioned slightly left of the midclavicular line, just below the costal margin.

The surgeon approaches the proximal stomach, with the short gastric vessels already divided. The hernia sac is further dissected, with the assistant holding at the base of the anterior vagus nerve visible in the top center of the screen. The surgeon divides the hernia sac up to the insertion of the vagus onto the proximal stomach, ensuring the anterior vagus nerve remains intact. The pronounced hernia sac and anterior gastric fat pad are carefully separated from the proximal stomach.

The surgeon has freed the hernia sac, which the assistant is now removing from the patient. The gastroesophageal junction is visible in the mediastinum, indicating the need for further mobilization of the esophagus to achieve adequate intra-abdominal length. The surgeon has repositioned the third arm anteriorly in the hiatus and begins the mediastinal dissection, focusing on the anterior third from ten to two o'clock on the esophagus. The dissection progresses to the left posterolateral third, taking care to avoid the pleura, and then to the right posterolateral third, where the esophageal mesentery is visible.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the esophageal aortic mesentery, visible in the bottom right of the screen alongside the thoracic spine vertebral bodies. The pleura is pointed out, and care is taken to avoid entering it. The aorta is seen adjacent to the thoracic spine, and robotic arms provide enhanced visualization and access to the mediastinum, aiding in achieving adequate intra-abdominal esophageal length.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to retract the retroesophageal window and places an Ethibond suture in a figure-of-eight fashion to re-approximate the posterior crura. The crura appear thin, suggesting reinforcement may be needed. The esophagus is seen gently passing over the posterior crural closure. A blunt tip Hurst bougie is prepared, with size tailored to the patient's body habitus. A Phasix ST mesh, cut with a U-shaped notch, is prepared for placement.

The surgeon tacks the Phasix ST mesh in place using interrupted Oethaban sutures, starting anteriorly and then placing another tack in the retroesophageal window where the crura were re-approximated. The mesh is positioned to reinforce the thin and attenuated crura, with additional sutures placed at the edges to keep it flat and prevent folding.

The surgeon makes relaxing incisions on the left hemidiaphragm due to excessive tension, covering the opening with nonabsorbable plastic mesh. The mesh is secured superiorly and inferiorly, with an anterior gap remaining to be addressed. A blunt-tipped bougie is placed in the proximal stomach, and the assistant uses a Babcock to grasp the fundus, performing a shoeshine maneuver to ensure the wrap is not twisted and has adequate mobility. The surgeon takes bites of the fundus and anterior esophagus to perform a Nissen fundoplication, ensuring generous intra-abdominal esophageal length.

The surgeon uses robotic tools to measure a distance of four centimeters from the left crus to the angle of His. Interrupted Oetaban sutures are used to create the wrap without Pledgets. The middle stitch is placed, followed by a lower stitch near the GE junction without involving the esophagus. The slipknot technique is employed for knot tying. The third wrap stitch is positioned above the first, ensuring a good bite of the stomach while avoiding hernia sac or residual fat. The stitches are kept within a two-centimeter total distance to prevent a long wrap. After placing the third stitch, the assistant removes the bougie, and anterior coronoplasty stitches are applied to close the remaining anterior gap.

The surgeon uses mesh to reinforce the coronal closure, ensuring it is not too tight to prevent postoperative dysphagia. Minimal visible gap is left to avoid complications. Interior gastropexy stitches are placed from the fundus to the left cruz and another from the right crease to the right side of the wrap, securing the fundus to the hiatus to minimize recurrence risk. The reconstruction of the hiatus and fundoplication is completed.

The surgeon checks for hemostasis, noting the surgical field is dry. The liver is lowered, and a small grasping retractor is removed through the liver retractor port. A XV French drain is placed into the mediastinum to manage potential seroma fluid, tucked under the liver and exiting through an existing port site. The surgeon performs intraoperative endoscopy to verify the wrap is not twisted and documents the hill grade of the valve, observing a hill grade one valve appearance, resembling a stack of coins or omega valve. The procedure concludes with the desufflation of the stomach.
